Ahmed Nasser
Hadoop Developer
ahmed.nasser@email.com |+20 100 123 4567 |/githubgithub.com/ahmed-nasser |/linkedinlinkedin.com/in/ahmed-nasser |

Education
Pune University

Experience
Hadoop Developer
PRGX India Private Limited Pune, India
Projects
Big Data Analytics for Banking Sector
PRGX India Private Limited Pune, India •Team Size: 10+
•Environment: Hive, Spark, Sqoop, Scala and Flume.
•Project Description: The bank wanted to help its customers to avail different products of the bank through analyzing
their expenditure behavior. The customers spending ranges from online shopping, medical expenses in hospitals,
cash transactions, and debit card usage etc. The behavior allows the bank to create an analytical report and based
on which the bank used to display the product offers on the customer portal which was built using Java. The portal
allows the customers to login and see their transactions which they make on a day to day basis. The analytics also
help the customers plan their budgets through the budget watch and my financial forecast applications embedded
into the portal. The portal used Hadoop framework to analyze the data as per the rules and regulations placed by
the regulators from the respective countries. The offers and the interest rates also complied with the regulations and
all these processing was done using the Hadoop framework as big data analytics system.
•Role & Responsibilities:
•• Import data from legacy system to Hadoop using Sqoop, Flume.
•• Implement the business logic to analyze the data.
•• Pre-process data using Spark.
•• Create Hive script and loading data into Hive.
•• Sourcing various attributes to the data processing logic to retrieve the correct results.
Web Application Data Extraction and Processing
PRGX India Private Limited Pune, India •Team Size: 11+
•Environment: Hadoop, HDFS, Hive, Sqoop, MySQL, Map Reduce.
•Project Description: The purpose of this project is to store terabytes of information from the web application and
extract meaningful information out of it. The solution was based on the open source software Hadoop. The data
will be stored in Hadoop file system and processed using Map/Reduce jobs. Which in turn includes getting the
raw HTML data from the micro websites, process the HTML to obtain product and user information, extract various
reports out of the visitor tracking information and export the information for further processing.
•Role & Responsibilities:
•• Move all crawl data flat files generated from various micro sites to HDFS for further processing.
•• Sqoop implementation for interaction with database.
•• Write Map Reduce scripts to process the data file.
•• Create Hive tables to store the processed data in tabular formats.
•• Reports creation from Hive data.
De

velopment Data Repository for Pfizer Inc.
PRGX India Private Limited Pune, India •Team Size: 15+
•Environment: Informatica 9.5, Oracle 11g, UNIX.

•Project Description: Pfizer Inc. is an American global pharmaceutical corporation headquartered in New York City.
The main objective of the project is to build a Development Data Repository (DDR) for Pfizer Inc. Because all the
downstream application are like Etrack, TSP database, RTS, SADMS, GFS, GDO having their own SQL request on
the OLTP system directly due to which the performance of OLTP system goes slows down. For this we have created
a Development Data Repository to replace the entire SQL request directly on the OLTP system. DDR process extracts
all clinical, pre-clinical, study, product, subject, sites related information from the upstream applications like EPECS,
CDSS, RCM, PRC, E-CLINICAL, EDH and after applying some business logic put it into DDR core tables. From
these snapshot and dimensional layer are created which are used for reporting application.
•Role & Responsibilities:
•• To understand & analyze the requirement documents and resolve the queries.
•• To design Informatica mappings by using various basic transformations like Filter, Router, Source qualifier, Lookup
etc. and advance transformations like Aggregators, Joiner, Sorters and so on.
•• Perform cross Unit and Integration testing for mappings developed within the team. Reporting bugs and bug
fixing.
•• Create workflow/batches and set the session dependencies.
•• Implemented Change Data Capture using mapping parameters, SCD and SK generation.
•• Developed Mapplet, reusable transformations to populate the data into data warehouse.
•• Created Sessions & Worklets using workflow Manager to load the data into the Target Database.
•• Involved in Unit Case Testing (UTC).
•• Performing Unit Testing and UAT for SCD Type1/Type2, fact load and CDC implementation.
Certificates
Skills
Big Data Ecosystems: Hadoop - HDFS - HBase - Map Reduce - Sqoop - Hive - Pig - Spark-Core - Flume
Programming Languages: Scala - Core-Java - SQL - PLSQL - Shell Scripting
ETL Tools: Informatica Power Center 8.x/9.6 - Talend 5.6
Development Tools: Eclipse - Intellij Idea
Platforms: Windows Family - Linux /UNIX - Cloudera
Databases: MySQL - Oracle 10/11g
Extracurricular Activities

Ahmed Nasser is a Hadoop Developer with contact information including email (ahmed.nasser@email.com), phone number (+20 100 123 4567), and links to his GitHub and LinkedIn profiles, as well as a personal website (ahmednasser.com).
Ahmed Nasser received an M.C.A. degree from Pune University in Pune, India.

Ahmed Nasser worked as a Hadoop Developer at PRGX India Private Limited in Pune, India, on a project involving Big Data Analytics for the Banking Sector with a team size of 10+ people. The project utilized Hive, Spark, Sqoop, Scala, and Flume to analyze customer expenditure behavior and provide personalized product offers through a Java-built portal.

Ahmed Nasser was involved in importing data using Sqoop and Flume, implementing business logic, pre-processing data with Spark, creating Hive scripts, and sourcing attributes for data processing within the Big Data Analytics for Banking Sector project.

Ahmed Nasser worked on a project at PRGX India Private Limited in Pune, India, involving Web Application Data Extraction and Processing, with a team size of 11+. The project utilized Hadoop, HDFS, Hive, Sqoop, MySQL, and Map Reduce to store and process terabytes of web application data, extracting product and user information and generating reports.

Ahmed Nasser was responsible for moving crawl data to HDFS, implementing Sqoop for database interaction, writing Map Reduce scripts, creating Hive tables, and generating reports from Hive data in the Web Application Data Extraction and Processing project.

The project involves building a Development Data Repository (DDR) for Pfizer Inc. to improve the performance of the OLTP system by replacing direct SQL requests from downstream applications like Etrack, TSP database, RTS, SADMS, GFS, and GDO. The DDR extracts clinical, pre-clinical, study, product, subject, and site-related information from upstream applications such as EPECS, CDSS, RCM, PRC, E-CLINICAL, and EDH, applying business logic before storing it in DDR core tables.

The role and responsibilities include understanding and analyzing requirements, designing Informatica mappings using various transformations, performing cross-unit and integration testing, creating workflows and batches, implementing Change Data Capture (CDC) using mapping parameters and SCD, developing reusable transformations, creating sessions and worklets, and performing unit and UAT testing for SCD Type1/Type2, fact load, and CDC implementation.

The individual possesses skills in Big Data Ecosystems (Hadoop, HDFS, HBase, MapReduce, Sqoop, Hive, Pig, Spark-Core, Flume), Programming Languages (Scala, Core-Java, SQL, PLSQL, Shell Scripting), ETL Tools (Informatica Power Center 8.x/9.6, Talend 5.6), Development Tools (Eclipse, Intellij Idea), Platforms (Windows Family, Linux/UNIX, Cloudera), and Databases (MySQL, Oracle 10/11g).

UNIX is a seasoned, quietly powerful presence – a foundational platform upon which much modern data infrastructure is built. Not flashy, but relentlessly reliable, it’s the bedrock for complex operations, particularly within environments demanding high performance and scalability. Think of it as the experienced engineer who doesn’t need to boast, but whose work underpins everything. 

This isn’t a system for casual use; it’s a workspace for those who speak its language – Scala, Core-Java, SQL, PLSQL, and the concise commands of Shell Scripting. It comfortably hosts sprawling ecosystems like Hadoop, HDFS, HBase, and Spark-Core, acting as a stable base for Big Data’s ambitious projects. It’s equally at home with established databases like MySQL and Oracle, and proven ETL tools like Informatica and Talend. 

In the context of Pfizer’s Development Data Repository (DDR), UNIX is the silent facilitator, the ground where data flows from upstream sources – EPECS, CDSS, RCM, and others – is processed, and ultimately, where the DDR core tables find their home. It’s the platform enabling the extraction of vital clinical and study information, the engine powering the shift away from direct SQL requests and towards a more efficient, centralized data system. It’s comfortable alongside Windows, but thrives in environments demanding robust control and adaptability, a testament to its enduring relevance.

Hive is a crucial component within the bustling world of Big Data, a tireless worker in the processing and analysis of massive datasets. Often found alongside companions like Hadoop, Spark, and Sqoop, Hive excels at providing a SQL-like interface to query data stored in distributed storage – particularly HDFS. It’s a tool favored for projects demanding the dissection of complex information, as evidenced by its role in both a Banking Sector analytics initiative and a Web Application Data Extraction project. 

In the banking context, Hive helped unlock insights into customer spending habits, fueling personalized product recommendations. Within web application processing, it transformed terabytes of crawl data into actionable reports, revealing patterns in product and user behavior. Hive doesn’t build or present; it *reveals* – structuring chaos into comprehensible tables and facilitating the extraction of valuable intelligence. 

It’s a foundational element for those skilled in the broader Big Data ecosystem, a key instrument in the hands of developers like Ahmed Nasser, who expertly crafted Hive scripts to source attributes and generate reports, bridging the gap between raw data and meaningful conclusions. Hive isn’t flashy, but it’s relentlessly effective, a quiet engine powering data-driven decisions.

Sqoop is a tool of quiet power, a bridge builder in the sprawling landscape of Big Data. Not flashy, but relentlessly practical, it specializes in the reliable and efficient transfer of data between Hadoop – the vast, distributed storage system – and structured datastores like MySQL and Oracle. Think of it as a seasoned logistics expert, expertly moving terabytes of information with precision and control. 

It’s a key component within the larger Big Data Ecosystem, working alongside tools like Hive, Spark, and Flume. While others focus on processing and analysis, Sqoop handles the crucial initial and final steps: getting data *into* Hadoop for analysis, and sending refined results *back out* to relational databases. 

Individuals like Ahmed Nasser, a Hadoop Developer, rely on Sqoop’s capabilities to fuel data-driven projects. Nasser utilized Sqoop to import data for a project analyzing customer spending habits in the banking sector, and again to connect to databases during the extraction and processing of massive web application datasets. It’s a tool favored when raw data needs taming, when the wealth of information locked within traditional databases needs to be unleashed for the power of Big Data analytics. Sqoop doesn’t *transform* the data, it simply *moves* it, making it a foundational element for larger data pipelines.

India is a land of ancient history and burgeoning technological prowess, a nation where tradition and innovation intertwine. It’s a vast and diverse country, home to vibrant cities like Pune, a significant hub for education and the tech industry. Pune University, a respected institution within India, serves as a launching pad for ambitious minds like Ahmed Nasser, who honed his skills in Master of Computer Applications there. 

India’s economic landscape is increasingly shaped by data and analytics, attracting global companies like Pfizer who seek to leverage its skilled workforce. Within India, companies like PRGX India Private Limited are at the forefront of this revolution, tackling complex data challenges for major players in sectors like banking and pharmaceuticals. PRGX India, specifically in Pune, has become a crucible for big data expertise, employing teams of ten or more developers. 

These teams, utilizing cutting-edge technologies – Hadoop, Hive, Spark, Scala, Sqoop, Flume, MapReduce, MySQL – are tasked with extracting meaning from massive datasets. They build systems to understand customer behavior, personalize offerings, and streamline data access. One such project focuses on creating a Development Data Repository (DDR) for Pfizer, a critical infrastructure designed to improve the performance of their systems by consolidating and refining clinical and research data from a multitude of upstream applications – EPECS, CDSS, RCM, and others. This DDR, built with meticulous business logic, promises to accelerate insights and improve efficiency for Pfizer’s global operations, all originating from the intellectual capital within India. India, therefore, isn’t merely a location, but an active participant in the global flow of information and innovation.

PRGX India Private Limited, based in Pune, is a technology services company specializing in large-scale data solutions. They operate as a key partner for organizations requiring sophisticated data processing and analytical capabilities, notably serving the banking and pharmaceutical sectors. The company thrives on tackling complex data challenges, demonstrated by projects involving terabyte-scale web application data and the creation of robust Development Data Repositories. 

Their expertise lies in the Hadoop ecosystem – encompassing technologies like Hive, Spark, Sqoop, and MapReduce – alongside traditional databases like MySQL and programming languages such as Scala and Java. PRGX India doesn’t simply store data; they transform it, applying crucial business logic to extract meaningful insights. A recent undertaking for Pfizer involved building a DDR to streamline data access for critical applications, improving system performance by consolidating information from numerous upstream sources. 

They foster collaborative teams, often exceeding ten members, and attract skilled professionals like Hadoop Developers focused on delivering data-driven solutions – from personalized product offers based on customer expenditure analysis to comprehensive reporting on user and product behavior. PRGX India positions itself as a vital link in the data pipeline, bridging the gap between raw information and actionable intelligence.

Pune is a vibrant Indian city, a nucleus for education and a growing tech hub. Nestled within India, it’s a place where academic pursuits flourish, evidenced by the presence of Pune University – a cornerstone for aspiring technologists like Ahmed Nasser, who earned his M.C.A. there. But Pune isn’t merely academic; it’s a practical proving ground for innovation. 

The city hosts companies like PRGX India Private Limited, a significant player in the data analytics field. Within PRGX, individuals like Nasser contribute to large-scale projects tackling complex data challenges. He’s been instrumental in projects leveraging the power of Big Data – utilizing tools like Hive, Spark, and Scala to decipher customer spending patterns for the banking sector, ultimately personalizing product offerings. He’s also worked on extracting valuable insights from massive streams of web application data, employing Hadoop and related technologies to build comprehensive reports. 

More recently, Pune-based teams at PRGX have undertaken projects of critical importance to global pharmaceutical giants like Pfizer, constructing Development Data Repositories (DDRs) designed to streamline data access and improve system performance. These DDRs act as central hubs, consolidating clinical and research data from various source applications, applying crucial business logic, and ultimately providing a more efficient data landscape. Pune, therefore, isn’t just a location; it’s a dynamic ecosystem where data, technology, and innovation converge, shaping solutions for industries worldwide.

Oracle 11g is a robust and mature relational database management system, a cornerstone of Pfizer’s Development Data Repository (DDR). It serves as the central storage for a wealth of critical data – clinical trial results, pre-clinical findings, study details, product information, and subject/site specifics – meticulously extracted from a complex network of upstream systems like EPECS, CDSS, and RCM. Though newer database technologies exist, 11g’s proven stability and capacity make it well-suited for this demanding role. 

It isn’t a solitary player, however. Oracle 11g is the destination for data refined by ETL processes, often orchestrated through Informatica Power Center or Talend, and is frequently accessed via SQL and PL/SQL. Its purpose within the DDR is strategic: to offload the burden of direct data requests from operational systems – Etrack, TSP, RTS, and others – improving their performance and responsiveness. While comfortable within a traditional database environment, it exists alongside a broader skillset encompassing Big Data technologies like Hadoop and Spark, suggesting a capacity to integrate with more modern data architectures. It’s a reliable workhorse, a seasoned veteran in the data landscape, quietly powering critical decision-making at Pfizer.

Ahmed Nasser is a resourceful Hadoop Developer, a digital architect building solutions from the ground up. Educated with an M.C.A. from Pune University, he thrives in the world of Big Data, possessing a practical skillset honed through projects at PRGX India Private Limited. He’s readily accessible – a quick email to ahmed.nasser@email.com, a call to +20 100 123 4567, or a connection via LinkedIn (linkedin.com/in/ahmed-nasser) or GitHub (github.com/ahmed-nasser) will find a dedicated professional. His personal website, ahmednasser.com, offers further insight into his work.

Nasser isn’t simply a coder; he’s a data pipeline builder. He’s adept at extracting value from massive datasets, demonstrated by his work on projects handling terabytes of information. In the Banking Sector project, he was a key player in a team of ten, leveraging Hive, Spark, Sqoop, Scala, and Flume to analyze customer spending habits and personalize product offerings through a Java portal. He’s comfortable with the entire process – from importing data with Sqoop and Flume, to pre-processing with Spark, crafting Hive scripts, and ultimately, sourcing the attributes needed for insightful data processing. 

He also contributed significantly to a Web Application Data Extraction and Processing project, a team effort of eleven, where he moved crawl data to HDFS, implemented Sqoop for database interaction, and built MapReduce scripts to transform raw data into actionable reports generated from Hive. He’s fluent in the language of data – HDFS, Hive, Sqoop, MySQL, and MapReduce are all tools in his arsenal, used to unlock hidden patterns and deliver valuable intelligence. He’s a pragmatic developer, focused on delivering tangible results from complex data challenges.

Flume is a diligent data collector, a crucial component in the bustling world of Big Data. Not glamorous, perhaps, but utterly essential – a tireless worker responsible for gathering vast streams of information and delivering it reliably to its destination. Within PRGX India’s Banking Sector project, Flume served as a key importer, working alongside Sqoop to bring raw customer expenditure data into the analytical pipeline. It doesn’t *process* the data, not directly; instead, it’s the steady hand that ensures a constant, unbroken flow. 

Ahmed Nasser, a Hadoop Developer, understood Flume’s quiet strength, skillfully employing it to feed the larger analytical engine built with Hive, Spark, and Scala. Flume isn’t a standalone tool, but a vital link – a conduit within the broader Big Data Ecosystem, alongside Hadoop, HDFS, and HBase. It’s a foundational element, quietly enabling insights into customer behavior and powering personalized product offers. Though unseen by the end user, Flume’s consistent performance is the bedrock upon which data-driven decisions are made. It’s a workhorse, built for volume and reliability, and thrives in the complex landscape of large-scale data processing.
